affordances.name: Closed-loop action signal
affordances.mechanism: When advice depends on learning from reality, require the answer to name the observable consequence, the signal timing, the lever that can change next, and the behavior that will be adjusted when feedback arrives.

use_when: Outcomes should shape the next action.
Behavior changes the environment and the environment changes behavior back.
Measurement can actually close the loop because signals are tied to real levers.
do_not_use_when: Feedback is collected but not used to change behavior, priorities, or design.
Delayed feedback, nonlinear relationships, or local bounded rationality make the visible signal misleading.
The team keeps repeating the same move while calling measurement a feedback loop.
case_evidence_needed: The action or intervention that will produce feedback.
The observable consequence and when it will arrive.
The lever that can change after the signal is read.
The behavioral adjustment that will close the loop.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to define the feedback signal, arrival window, decision lever, and next behavior change so measurement becomes a loop rather than passive reporting.
evidence_required: The observed consequence or feedback metric.
The timing and delay of the signal.
The next adjustment that the signal could trigger.
good_output_shape: Links it to a real lever.
Defines what behavior, priority, or design will change.

diagnostic_questions: What feedback signal will arrive soon enough to change the next action?
Which lever will change when the signal arrives?
Are we collecting feedback or actually closing the loop?
misuse_guards: Do not instrument the system heavily without changing behavior.
Do not ignore delays or nonlinear effects in the feedback signal.
Do not call measurement a loop if no lever changes afterward.

source_evidence.source_quote: outcomes should shape the next action
measurement can actually close the loop
signals are tied to real levers, so the next adjustment can be made on the basis of what the system just produced

review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 8 outcome: strong_affordance_record. The source supports one closed-loop action signal requiring observable consequences, real levers, timing, and changed behavior.
dropped_material: PR57 promotes loop polarity as a separate bounded affordance; digital twins and creative iteration remain implementation contexts rather than standalone cards.
open_questions: PR40 should test whether this card prevents packets from treating reporting as learning.

affordances.name: Loop polarity intervention map
affordances.mechanism: When output feeds back into input and the intervention depends on system behavior, require the answer to classify the loop as balancing or reinforcing, then choose whether to stabilize, dampen, amplify, or monitor delayed nonlinear effects.

use_when: A system grows, stabilizes, oscillates, or runs away because output feeds back into input.
Repeated behavior suggests a reinforcing vicious or virtuous cycle rather than a one-way causal chain.
The right intervention depends on whether the loop is stabilizing, goal-seeking, self-enhancing, or runaway.
do_not_use_when: The case only needs a measurement signal tied to a next lever; use the closed-loop action affordance instead.
There is no evidence that system output is acting as input to the same system.
Delay, nonlinearity, or missing boundary information makes loop polarity too uncertain to classify confidently.
case_evidence_needed: The system variable or output that feeds back into future input.
The feedback path linking output to the next system state.
Evidence for balancing, stabilizing, reinforcing, compounding, or runaway behavior.
The intervention lever and whether it should stabilize, dampen, amplify, or wait for delayed effects.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to map the feedback path, classify loop polarity, and choose an intervention consistent with stabilizing or reinforcing dynamics.
evidence_required: The output or stock that changes over time.
How that output feeds back into the system.
The evidence for balancing or reinforcing polarity.
The lever, delay, or boundary condition affecting intervention choice.
good_output_shape: Classifies the loop as balancing or reinforcing, with uncertainty if needed.
Chooses stabilize, dampen, amplify, or observe based on polarity.
States the delay or nonlinear caveat that could change the intervention.

diagnostic_questions: Is the loop pushing the system back toward equilibrium or amplifying movement away from it?
What output becomes the next input, and through which path?
Would the intervention dampen a vicious cycle, restore a balancing target, or amplify a useful compounding loop?
misuse_guards: Do not label every repeated pattern a reinforcing loop without an output-to-input path.
Do not intervene on a loop before deciding whether the target is stability, damping, or amplification.
Do not ignore delays and nonlinear effects when judging loop polarity.

source_evidence.source_quote: These are stabilizing, goal-seeking, and regulating mechanisms that oppose or reverse the direction of change imposed on the system.
These are self-enhancing loops that lead to exponential growth or runaway collapses over time.
Feedback loops are foundational structures in systems; they determine if the system grows or stabilizes.
that reinforcing loop becomes dominant, causing the system to get out of balance or leading to a "vicious cycle" of overthinking that feels like progress but is not.

review_notes.normalization_note: PR57 targeted enrichment: the source supports loop polarity and intervention choice as a separate receiver transaction from measurement-to-action closure.
dropped_material: Did not promote digital twins, causal-loop diagram tooling, or generic creative iteration as separate affordances.
Kept delay, nonlinearity, and boundary uncertainty as guards on polarity classification.
open_questions: Packet stress should check whether this card routes only cases with circular causality, not ordinary KPI feedback.

affordances.name: Absolute standard drift guard
affordances.mechanism: When feedback is being used to explain lower results, require the answer to separate legitimate learning from standard drift by naming the original standard, the recent signal, the proposed target change, and the process lever that should change without normalizing weaker performance.

use_when: Recent feedback or poor performance is being used to quietly lower the accepted standard.
The decision depends on whether the team should adapt the process, the lever, or the target itself.
The system needs feedback-informed adjustment while keeping objective standards from drifting downward.
do_not_use_when: The original standard is obsolete because the environment, goal, or constraint genuinely changed.
The case only needs a feedback signal tied to the next lever, with no evidence of target or standard drift.
The feedback signal is too delayed, nonlinear, or local to justify changing either the process or the standard yet.
case_evidence_needed: The original standard, threshold, or quality bar.
The recent feedback, poor result, or performance signal putting pressure on the standard.
The proposed target change and whether it reflects learning or drift.
The process, lever, or operating behavior that could change while the standard remains explicit.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to preserve the absolute standard unless the source evidence supports a legitimate standard change, and to route poor feedback into process adjustment rather than quiet normalization.
evidence_required: The standard or metric that is being lowered or defended.
The feedback signal and recent performance context.
The reason the standard should remain absolute or be explicitly revised.
The process lever, priority, or behavior that should change in response to the signal.
good_output_shape: Separates legitimate learning from drift to low performance.
States whether the target is being revised or preserved.
Identifies the process lever that changes before weaker performance becomes normal.

diagnostic_questions: Are we learning from feedback, or lowering the bar because recent performance was poor?
What standard was absolute before this feedback arrived?
Which process lever should change before the target itself is relaxed?
misuse_guards: Do not treat poor recent results as permission to normalize lower quality.
Do not freeze an obsolete standard when the environment or goal has genuinely changed.
Do not call a target change feedback-driven unless the signal and process implication are explicit.

source_evidence.source_quote: A major failure mode in systems is the slow acceptance of diminishing quality.
Explicitly define and adhere to objective, high standards for the system, regardless of recent poor results
What core standards have we failed to meet in the past, and how are we ensuring those same metrics remain absolute this quarter?

review_notes.normalization_note: PR93 targeted enrichment: the source supports standard drift prevention as a distinct receiver transaction from closing a loop or classifying loop polarity.
dropped_material: Did not promote all performance-management or goal-setting material; this affordance only activates when feedback pressure is lowering standards.
Kept obsolete-standard risk as a do-not-use boundary rather than a separate positive card.
open_questions: Packet stress should test whether this card prevents feedback language from normalizing degraded performance.

absence_records.reason: The source rejects feedback collection that never translates into changed behavior, priorities, or design.

source_evidence.source_quote: teams instrument the system heavily, yet never translate the signal into changed behavior, priorities, or design

absence_records.reason: The source warns that delays and nonlinear relationships are inherent in dynamic systems, so PR39 should not imply every signal is immediate or linear.

source_evidence.source_quote: delays** (the length of time relative to the rates of system changes) and **nonlinear relationships** are inherent properties

absence_records.reason: The source says feedback volume requires filtering; it does not support treating more feedback as better feedback unless the useful signal is identified.

source_evidence.source_quote: The sheer volume and variety of feedback we receive daily require a critical ability to **filter feedback** and identify which information is actually useful for progress.

absence_records.reason: The source warns that one-way communication creates stuck routines and misinformation, so PR93 must not promote broadcast-only reporting as a feedback loop.

source_evidence.source_quote: communication flows only in one direction, the system gets bogged down in routines and misinformation

absence_records.reason: The source warns that local logic can become unreasonable from the whole-system view; a local feedback signal should not be promoted as whole-system truth without broader context.

source_evidence.source_quote: logic that makes sense within a local part of a system but is unreasonable when viewed within the broader context or the entire system
the output of a system also acts as an input to that same system

review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 8 source reading found strong reviewed depth for Feedback Loops. PR57 added loop polarity intervention as a second, source-backed transaction; PR93 adds standard-drift prevention where feedback pressure lowers the bar rather than changing the process.
dropped_material: No runtime, prompt, lane, packet, or user-facing change is implied.
open_questions: Later execution-packet review should test whether this card makes feedback actionable rather than decorative.
